coredata.dc:title: Exfoliated graphitic carbon dots: Application in heavy metal ion sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Ansi, V.A.
Renuka, N.K.
coredata.dc:description: Highly Exfoliated Graphitic Carbon dot (HEG carbon dot) is reported herein, exploiting commercially available table sugar (sucrose) as a green precursor. An amorphous carbon matrix, made of 7–8 graphenic units constitutes the HEG carbon dot core. This system exhibits excitation energy transfer with the Xanthene dye, Rhodamine-B, which is expressed as a noticeable enhancement in the luminescence of the dye. Metal ion assisted hindrance in FRET is noticed in presence of Hg (II) ion, which paves way to the fabrication of an efficient Hg (II) ion sensing tool. The system exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity with Limit of Detection 98 pM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Highly Exfoliated Graphitic Carbon dots
Hg (II) sensing
Excitation energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon nanodot from table sugar
Synthesis of carbon nanodot from Honey
Structure of Table sugar derived carbon dots
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dot & Rhodamine-B
Rhodamine-B / carbon dot unit as Hg(II) ion sensor
Unveiling Hg (II) sensing action of nano hybrid –“Metal ion assisted hindrance in FRET”
Probing the practical applicability of present sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a); AFM image (b) of carbon dots. Reprinted from Sensors and actuators B: Chemical, 264, V.A Ansi, N.K Renuka, Table sugar derived Carbon dot – a naked eye sensor for toxic Pb2+ ions, 67–75, 2018, with permission from Elsevier).

XRD pattern (a) FTIR spectrum (b) of carbon dot; UV–Visible absorbance spectrum with digital image of blue luminescent solution captured under UV radiation with λexc350 nm (inset) (c) Fluorescence spectrum of carbon dot solution (d).
Schematic depiction of HEG Carbon dot core.
Modes of interactions between Rhodamine-B and Highly Exfoliated Graphitic core bearing carbon dot.
Excellent overlapping between fluorescence curve of donor and absorbance curve of acceptor (a) Jablonski diagram describing FRET between donor & acceptor (b).

FRET induced enhancement of fluorescence of Rhodamine solution upon the addition of carbon dot - Confirming the acceptor role of dye in the FRET process (a) Reduction in the intensity of carbon dot luminescence upon the addition of aliquots of Rhodamine solution (b)Fluorescence decay (life time) curve of carbon dot solution before and after Rhodamine addition(c).

XRD pattern (a) and UV absorbance curve (b) of honey derived C-dot; illustration of the absence of FRET interaction from Carbon dot to Rhodamine (c)Fluorescence curve of Rhodamine B solution upon introduction of honey derived carbon dot solution (d).

Fluorescence curve showing quenching of yellow emission of carbon dot- Rhodamine unit with the addition of Hg (II) ion (digital image showing decreased emission in the inset (a) Quantitative variation of fluorescence response with the concentration of Hg(II) ions (b).
Quenching profile of carbon dot- Rhodamine unit luminescence in presence of various metal ions, illustrating extreme selectivity towards Hg (II) ion.

Overlap of absorbance of Rhodamine and (a) emission of carbon dot (b) Carbon dot – Hg (II) mixture; UV absorbance curve of HEG-carbon dots upon the addition of Hg(II) ions showing significant interactions between Hg(II) ions and HEG-carbon dots (c).

Fluorescence response of carbon dot solution upon the introduction of aliquots of Hg(II) ion solution (demonstrating the blue shift of carbon dot fluorescence (a)Linearity of blue-shift with added Hg(II) ion concentrations (b).
Fluorescence response curves of Carbon dot- Rhodamine probe towards contaminated water (a) well water (b).
Some of the efficient Hg (II) sensors and their detection limits.

row.entry: Gold nanoparticles functionalized by dithiocarbamate derivative of calixarene [27]

Gold nanoparticles stabilized with a dithia-diaza ligand [31]

head.ce:title: Exfoliated graphitic carbon dots: Application in heavy metal ion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala 673635, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly Exfoliated Graphitic Carbon dot (HEG carbon dot) is reported herein, exploiting commercially available table sugar (sucrose) as a green precursor. An amorphous carbon matrix, made of 7–8 graphenic units constitutes the HEG carbon dot core. This system exhibits excitation energy transfer with the Xanthene dye, Rhodamine-B, which is expressed as a noticeable enhancement in the luminescence of the dye. Metal ion assisted hindrance in FRET is noticed in presence of Hg (II) ion, which paves way to the fabrication of an efficient Hg (II) ion sensing tool. The system exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity with Limit of Detection 98 pM.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nano dots, tiny spherical carbon particles of size less than 10 nm, are the fascinating materials in the carbon nano world, and research on these luminescent carbon moieties is in full swing globally. Countless number of reports on carbon dots prove amorphous 2D- graphitic carbon moieties in the core at greater inter planar distance than pure Graphite (~ 0.34 nm), which are decorated with surface functionalities. This noticeable gradation in ‘d’ spacing is sufficient enough to impart more non-graphitic mannerisms to carbon dots. Previous works regarding carbon dot structure investigations never took an attempt to unveil the structural pattern of the carbon core considering this increment in inter planar spacing. In one of the recent articles published in the journal of the American chemical society, Chan and co- workers have addressed carbon dots as “Nanometer-sized fragments of carbon in the form of multilayer graphene” [1], which suggests the presence of myriad graphenic units within the carbon dot core. Various synthetic routes including both top-down and bottom-up techniques are adopted for the synthesis of carbon dots. Among such synthetic routes, facile and green strategies such as simple hydrothermal/carbonization/microwave irradiation of natural precursors like orange peel [2], banana juice [3], sugar cane juice [4], Trapabispinosa peel [5], sugar cane bagasse [6] etc. have got extreme significance on account of their innocuous nature as well as cheap and time saving feature.

Herein, we report highly luminescent carbon nano particles derived from Table sugar, which exhibited Highly Exfoliated Graphitic (HEG) nature, denoted as HEG Carbon dots. Conventionally, the HEG structure is constituted by 5–15 Exfoliated Graphitic layers in 50 nm to few microns in size. Highly Exfoliated graphitic microplates (HEG microplates) & HEG nanoplates are already been identified and their composites are studied by the researchers. XRD analysis confirms the said structure which exhibited interplanar distance 0.37 nm corresponding to [002] plane that accounts for the distance between graphenic layers. The [101] plane with d spacing 0.2 nm is also noticed [7], confirming the Exfoliated Graphitic structures, and thus suggesting the presence of graphenic sheets within the core [8]. Graphene dots (GDs), made of 2–3 layered graphenic array also fall under the category of carbon nanodots. However, the disk like morphology with size 12–30 nm, keeps the former at a distance from HEG carbon dots. Maillard reaction assisted pathway is suggested to be the synthesis route to these dots [9]. In order to rule out the possibility of contamination in table sugar, chemically 100% pure sucrose also was tested for these studies. It is interesting to note that sucrose derived carbon dots prepared under identical conditions displayed exactly the same characteristic feature.

The HEG carbon dots enabled optical detection of Hg (II) ions with extraordinary selectivity, when combined with the xanthene dye, Rhodamine-B, which may be attributed to the interaction between the dye and graphenic layers [10]. Hg (II) is one of the most toxic species, the admissible limit of which is fixed as 2 ppb in aqueous medium. It is highly hazardous in all its oxidation states, notwithstanding possible redox interconversion and differences in solubility [11–15]. Among these, Hg2+ is one of the most abundant and stable mercury forms, and the adverse effect of this ion is already known to all. The extreme lethal nature of Hg (II) ions escalates the demand of an efficient sensing tool with high detection ability. Researchers mainly suggest organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots and noble metal nanoparticle architectures for the sensing of Hg2+ ions. In this direction, Au and Ag nanoparticles have gained credible attention among the fluorescent probes [16,17]. The system reported herein (HEG carbon dots) exhibits limit of detection (LOD) 98 picomolar (~ 20ppt). Even though the LOD value offered by the Au nanoporous /Rhodamine 6 G unit [17] is superior to the present system, the extreme facile, green and economic nature of the latter outweighs it from the former. This noteworthy ability of the system is attributed to Excitation Energy Transfer from carbon dot to Rhodamine. This is further confirmed by preparing a carbon dot system using honey as the precursor which does not exhibit this Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) ability.

Table sugar used as precursor of carbon dots was obtained from the local market. 95% ammonia solution was purchased from Merck. Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscope was used for HRTEM analysis. X-ray diffraction studies were carried out with the aid of RigakuMiniFlex-II using Cu K
(
α
)
radiation. UV assisted characterizations were carried out using LUZ chem. LZC-4X photo reactor. JASCO FTIR-4100 spectrometer was used for the FTIR analysis by KBr disk method. JASCO V-550 spectrophotometer was utilized for UV–Visible absorbance spectra measurement. Photoluminescence spectra were obtained from Perkin Elmer Fluorescence spectrometer LS 55.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon nanodot from table sugar
ce:section.ce:para: About 1 g of table sugar was calcined at 150° Cand the slurry formed was added to 20 ml of water containing 5 ml of ammonia solution. The mixture was subjected to ultra-sonication for 12 h to obtain carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon nanodot from Honey
ce:section.ce:para: About 2 g honey was made into a solution with distilled water and stirred to get a uniform solution. After adding requisite amount of H2O2, the solution was kept in Teflon lined autoclave at 200 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace to obtain a clear transparent solution. The white powder obtained after freeze drying was subjected to further characterizations.
Stock solution of Rhodamine-B dye was prepared by dissolving requisite amount of Rhodamine-B dye in ethanol as solvent. pH of the solution was found to be around 4.2.

Solutions of different metal ions (Hg2+, Pb2+,Cd2+,Cu2+,Zn2+ Cu2+,Fe3+, Ba2+,Na+, K+ and Ni2+) in the form of chlorides and nitrates were prepared by dissolving required amounts of metal salt in deionized water so as to obtain 100 µm solution. The solution after proper dilution was used for the sensing studies. Deionized water was utilized as solvent throughout the experiments. The pH of the solution was found to be in neutral range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure of Table sugar derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) analysis of the system shows spherical nano architectures with diameter of 3–4 nm, confirming the formation of zero dimensional carbon dot structure ( Fig. 1a). Morphology of the present system is further studied using Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) analysis. Fig. 1(b) represents the AFM image of a dilute solution of HEG carbon dot, which supports the conclusion from the TEM analysis. An idea about the carbon core is achieved from the X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis. Fig. 2(a) displays the XRD pattern of the system. In addition to the peak at 24⁰, it also possessed a distinct peak at 44.5⁰. XRD peak at 24⁰ is due to the diffraction from (002) planes inherent of graphitic origin which represents the inter-planar distance between the graphenic layers. The corresponding spacing was 0.37 nm, which is significantly higher than pure graphitic carbon (0.34 nm). The peak at 44.5⁰ with d spacing 0.2 nm is assigned to (101) planes of 2D carbon layers, showing the higher order of the 2D array in vertical direction. The pattern is found to be comparable with the XRD pattern of Highly Exfoliated Graphite (HEG) system. Increased ‘d’ spacing of this HEG carbon dots compared to simple Graphite moiety also confirms the extreme exfoliation of Graphite layers, thus imparting graphenic character to carbon dots [8]. Combining the carbon dot size derived from TEM measurements and the interplanargraphenic layer distance, it is suggested that sucrose derived carbon dot is composed of 8 layers of graphenic units.

The FTIR spectrum of the system is shown in Fig. 2(b). The band at 1000 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of C-O-C epoxy functional group. Appearance of a broad band at 3400 cm−1 represents stretching vibrations of C-OH, indicative of abundant –OH groups on the core of carbon nanodots. In addition to these bands, bands around 1648 & 1423 cm−1 representing stretching vibrations of carboxylate groups were also seen. FTIR spectrum of the prepared carbon dots is thus marked by bands characteristic of functionalities that are inherent of graphenic layers such as epoxy, carboxyl etc. Fig. 2(c) shows the UV–Visible absorbance spectrum of prepared carbon dot solution covering a broad range of 250–400 nm. The carbon dot solution exhibits bright blue luminescence upon excitation with radiation of wavelength 350 nm (Fig. 2c inset). Fluorescence spectrum of carbon dot solution (Fig. 2d) reveals excellent emission by the carbon dot solution in 400–600 nm regions. This commendable Photoluminescence feature of carbon dots is often utilized for the selective sensing of certain important chemical entities like organic molecules, biological structures, toxic metal ions etc. The schematic representation of carbon core framework is given in Fig. 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dot & Rhodamine-B
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have reported the interaction between the xanthene dye Rhodamine and graphene moieties that leads to supramolecular assembly, enabling the fabrication of excellent metal ion sensors. The principle behind such sensors is observed to be the Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). FRET is known to be a non-radiative quantum mechanical process where energy transfer occurs between two fluorophores in close proximity (less than 10 nm apart) via long range dipole-dipole interactions [18,19]. One member in the pair acts as donor molecule and the other one assumes the role of acceptor, as a consequence of which the fluorescence intensity of the latter is increased. FRET based detection has become a powerful tool for quantitative measurements of various analytes in numerous fields [20–22]. Considerable amount of π-π and dispersive interactions between Rhodamine and the core of carbon dots ( Fig. 4) brings this donor-accepter pair within the desirable FRET distance [23]. In the present case, carbon dot is the donor and Rhodamine the acceptor, leading to increased luminescence intensity of the dye in presence of carbon dot.

Probability of FRET can be understood by the extent of overlapping of emission curve of the donor (carbon dots) with the absorbance curve of the acceptor, dye ( Fig. 5). This is represented in Fig. 6(a), which clearly reveals high probability for excellent FRET between them. Literature also supports this observation, as the donor role of carbon dot in xanthene dye- carbon dot system has already been established in the case of fluorescein [24]. Jablonski diagram describes the mechanism of energy transfer from the donor to the acceptor during the FRET process (Fig. 6b). As a consequence of excitation energy transfer, the yellow fluorescence of Rhodamine solution is found to be enhanced upon the addition of carbon dot solution ( Fig. 7a). Digital image displaying the gradation in the luminescence intensity of Rhodamine dye is shown in the inset. The concomitant reduction in the luminescence of carbon dot solution upon the addition of Rhodamine dye (Fig. 7b) confirms the donor action of carbon dot in the FRET process. In addition to this, emergence of luminescence peak corresponding to Rhodamine dye also can be observed in the spectrum at higher concentrations of dye addition. Digital images showing the aforesaid decrease in carbon dot emission is displayed in the inset. The decreased lifetime of carbon dot solution in presence of Rhodamine (2.43 ns) when compared to that of pure carbon dots (3.49 ns), as derived from Fig. 7(c) also supports this.

Other carbon dot system which doesn’t exhibit FRET with Rhodamine fails to display this sensing character. This is illustrated by comparing the response of honey derived carbon dots. Fig. 8(a) and (b) display the XRD pattern and the UV absorbance of the above mentioned system. Fig. 8(c) shows the nil chance for FRET from these C-dots to Rhodamine. Consequently the luminescence of the dye is not enhanced upon carbon dot addition (Fig. 8d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rhodamine-B / carbon dot unit as Hg(II) ion sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Different metal ion solutions were prepared at definite concentrations and were tested to see whether they evoke any noticeable change in the optical response of the fabricated carbon dot-dye unit. It was noted that Hg (II) ions do change the luminescence of carbon dot-Rhodamine solution. As evident from the figure, FRET induced enhanced emission profile is decreased upon the introduction of Hg(II) ions ( Fig. 9a). Noticeable reduction in luminescence intensity was observed as the concentration of Hg (II) ions is increased. Digital image displayed in the inset also justifies the reduction of luminescence. The variation of optical response of the sensor unit was directly proportional to the concentration of Hg 2+ ions, as indicated by Fig. 9(a). The linear correlation between fluorescence intensity of the Rhodamine - carbon dot system and the concentration of Hg2+ ion is represented in Fig. 9(b), which displayed regression coefficient R2 = 0.98.

The limit of detection, as obtained using standard deviation method (3 *σ/m) was found to be 98 picomolar (~20 ppt). The value worth comparison with the efficiency of most extolled Hg(II) sensors reported previously. As mentioned earlier, commendable Surface Plasmon Resonance trait inherent of Au based nano structures enables those systems to rule the Hg sensing field as preeminent and unparalleled candidates. Some of the efficient Au based Hg (II) sensors based on optical methods and their corresponding LOD values are listed in Table 1. Apart from these, various electroanalytical methods are widely adopted for tracing mercury ions in aqueous solutions [32–34]. Detection limit in picomolar level achieved using polymer immobilised horseradish peroxidase biosensor system via amperometry is worth mentioning at this context [35].

Selectivity /behaviour in presence of the other cations is an important aspect when a sensor is fabricated. Fig. 10 illustrates the extremely high selectivity of the system towards Hg(II) ions. In order to test the interference behaviour of all the test ions, the two test solutions (one set containing mixture of all the ions including Hg2+ ion and the second set with similar contents except Hg2+ ion) were screened for their interaction with the sensor unit. Interestingly, the mixture of the ions with Hg2+ ion was able to quench the optical response of carbon dot- Rhodamine unit, whereas the other mixture excluding Hg2+ ion failed in showing this response. The results thus confirm the reliability and commendable selectivity of the system towards Hg(II) ions even under complicated environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unveiling Hg (II) sensing action of nano hybrid –“Metal ion assisted hindrance in FRET”
ce:section.ce:para: The rate of energy transfer between the donor-acceptor pair is highly dependent on many parameters, such as the extent of spectral overlap, the relative orientation of transition dipoles and most importantly, their distance of separation. Since FRET is extremely sensitive to changes in relative positions of the two fluorophore or their orientations, even a subtle change in the conformation of the donor-acceptor pair can be visualized as a noticeable change in FRET efficiency. As mentioned earlier the drastic increment in Rhodamine luminescence is due to the excitation energy transfer ( FRET)from the donor carbon dot moiety. The addition of Hg (II) ions is sufficient enough to bring an evident interruption in the FRET interaction between Rhodamine and carbon dot moieties, which in turn results in the reduction of luminescence intensity of this nano hybrid. The noticeable quenching evident in the optical response of Carbon dot-Rhodamine nanohybrid upon the addition of Hg (II) ions, corroborate the “Metal ion assisted hindrance in FRET”. This concept of metal ion assisted hindrance in FRET is further substantiated by the decrease in the overlapping area of fluorescence curve of donor and absorbance curve of acceptor molecule upon the introduction of aliquots of Hg (II) ion to the FRET pair. Fig. 11 (a) depicts efficient energy transfer, as marked by larger area of overlapping of donor emission and acceptor absorbance curves. Significant reduction in the area of overlapping upon the introduction of mercury ions(20 ppt) indicates the decrease in efficient excitation energy transfer(Fig. 11 b).

This commendable affinity of Hg (II) ions towards the carbon dots structure, which is capable enough to segregate the well overlapped FRET interaction with Rhodamine B dye, can be attributed to the presence of numerous oxygen functionalities on the carbon dot core. Hg(II) ions exhibits remarkably specific and exceptional affinity towards functional groups like carboxyl (-COOH)and hydroxyl (-OH) groups over the graphenic layers. Fig. 11(c)shows the sequential reduction in absorbance corresponding to n to π * transition (arising from oxygen containing functionalities) with increasing amount of Hg (II), supporting this conclusion. In addition to this specific interaction,the interaction between cation and the π cloud also plays a role. This is supported by numerous studies, which reported highly specific interaction between graphene unit and Hg (II) ion, thus leading to efficient sensors. These strong interactions between carbondots and Hg (II) ions causes a blue shift in carbon dot emission, and hence disturb effective FRET from carbon dot to the dye, thereby reducing the emission of the nanohybrid. The blue-shift of carbon dot emission increased with the amount of Hg(II) ion, as evident from Fig. 12 (a), and the extent of blue shift varied linearly with concentration of added Hg(II) ions (Fig. 12 b). However the shift of emission to lower wavelength ceases at a particular concentration of Hg(II) ion beyond which no blue-shift was observed. This may be due to the lack of availability of further interaction sites in the carbon dots. It is clear that this itself can be regarded as a method for sensing Hg(II) ions,though in a smaller range. The above mentioned linear variation enabled the limit of detection value 5 ppm using the 3 * σ/m method, which of course is a comparatively high value. This interesting observation of blue-shift in presence of metal ion requires further deep probing on the energy levels of theses dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing the practical applicability of present sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the present carbon dot –Rhodamine unit is monitored by analyzing real water samples collected from nearby premises. Two water samples including well water and water contaminated with Hg (II) ions were checked for the presence of the analyte ions using the proposed sensor. Contaminated water was obtained by spiking the water sample with 50 ppt of Hg (II) ion. Fluorescence response of Carbon dot-Rhodamine probe is found to be quenched by the introduction of polluted water sample( Fig. 13 a), whereas water sample collected from well was found to be free from Hg (II) ions(Fig. 13 b). The amount of Hg(II) detected in the contaminated sample falls below 1.3 error percentage. This in turn proves the practical applicability of present system.

Ultra sound assisted sonication treatment of ammoniated table sugar (sucrose) slurry was carried out for the fabrication of carbon nano dot structures. Detailed investigation on the characteristic features of the synthesised system reveal a Highly Exfoliated graphitic (HEG) core of the system. The system was found to exhibit commendable FRET with Rhodamine-B dye. Introduction of Hg (II) ions into the FRET pair displaces donor moiety as a consequence of the extraordinary affinity of Hg (II) ions to carbon dots. The limit of detection observed for the sensing unit was 20 ppt, well below the permissible amount of Hg(II). The system is found to be potent enough to compete with other recognised noble metal based sensors too.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ansi V.A gratefully acknowledges the financial assistance received from the Government of Kerala, and the facilities received from the Department of Chemisty, University of Calicut.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.
